## Slide 1 {#slide-01}

**Chapter 13 New Biology**


## Slide 2 {#slide-02}

**Chapter 13 At a Glance**

- What Is Biotechnology?
- Polymerase Chain Reaction
- Forensics
- Human Genome Project

## Slide 3 {#slide-03}


## Slide 4 {#slide-04}

**DNA Technology Is Changing the World**

- DNA Technology’s Tools: Recombinant DNA, DNA sequencing, polymerase chain reaction (PCR), DNA profiling
- Using DNA Technology to Copy Cells and Organisms: Stem cell research, cloning
- Using DNA Technology in Medicine: DNA probes in preimplantation genetic diagnosis (PGD) and genetic testing, gene therapy

## Slide 5 {#slide-05}

**To catch a killer**

- Joseph James DeAngelo
- 13 victims 1974-1986

## Slide 6 {#slide-06}

**Scientist Spotlight: Kary Mullis**

- Won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry
- Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
- Clones genes
- Used in forensics, paternity testing, and genetic engineering

## Slide 7 {#slide-07}

**DNA Replication in a Tube**

- What if investigators only have a tiny amount of DNA to examine? It might be useful to replicate the DNA using the polymerase chain reaction (PCR).

## Slide 8 {#slide-08}

**PCR Replicates DNA in a Test Tube**

- First, DNA polymerase, nucleotides, and primers are combined with the target sequence of DNA.

## Slide 9 {#slide-09}

**PCR Step 1 - Denaturation**

- The mixture is heated, causing the DNA strands to separate.
- 95˚C
- This step breaks the hydrogen bonds between the strands of DNA.

## Slide 10 {#slide-10}

**PCR Step 2 - Annealing**

- When the mixture cools, primers attach to the naked DNA strands.
- 55˚C

## Slide 11 {#slide-11}

**Primers**

- Primers – small pieces of complementary to the gene sequences targeted for copying
- One primer is complementary to one strand of the double helix and the other primer is complementary to the other strand

## Slide 12 {#slide-12}

**PCR Step 3 Extension**

- DNA polymerase replicates the strand, starting at the primer.
- 72˚C

## Slide 13 {#slide-13}

**PCR Doubles the DNA**

- Two copies of the target sequence are produced in round 1 of PCR. Subsequent rounds produce more copies.

## Slide 14 {#slide-14}

**Now What?**

- We have a tube full of pieces of DNA. How do we make that useful.
- The answer is gel electrophoresis

## Slide 15 {#slide-15}

**Gel Electrophoresis**

- Gel electrophoresis separates DNA segments
- Mixtures of DNA fragments can be separated on the basis of size
- Gel electrophoresis is a technique used to spread out DNA fragments of varying lengths in a mixture

## Slide 16 {#slide-16}

**Running a gel**


## Slide 17 {#slide-17}

**Chapter 13 At a Glance**

- What Is Biotechnology?
- Polymerase Chain Reaction
- Forensics
- Human Genome Project

## Slide 18 {#slide-18}

**Who is your daddy?**


## Slide 19 {#slide-19}

**DNA Sequencing Reveals the Order of Bases**

- Researchers put the unknown DNA sequence, primers, nucleotides, and replication enzymes together. Replication occurs as it would in a cell.

## Slide 20 {#slide-20}

**Dye Labeled Nucleotides**

- Then, modified nucleotides are added to the mix. When these nucleotides are incorporated into the replicating strand of DNA, replication stops.

## Slide 21 {#slide-21}

**Electrophoresis of the Dye-Labeled Products**

- The samples now contain DNA strands of various lengths. They are transferred to an electrophoresis gel, which separates them by size.

## Slide 22 {#slide-22}

**Electrophoresis**

- The shortest strands travel the farthest in the gel.

## Slide 23 {#slide-23}

**Order of the bases is determined**

- The gel reveals the order of nucleotides in the newly created DNA, which is complementary to the original sequence.

## Slide 24 {#slide-24}

**Sequence**

- Fluorescent labels and computers make sequencing DNA simple.

## Slide 25 {#slide-25}

**DNA sequence shows how different organisms are unique**

- Most human DNA is “noncoding” – only about 1.5% of it encodes proteins.

## Slide 26 {#slide-26}

**How Is Biotechnology Used in Forensic Science?**

- Unrelated people almost never have identical DNA profiles
- DNA samples run on STR gels produce a pattern, called a DNA profile
- The positions of the bands on the gel are determined by the numbers of repeats in each STR allele
- If the same STRs are analyzed, then a particular DNA sample will produce the same profile every time

## Slide 27 {#slide-27}

**Who did it?**


## Slide 28 {#slide-28}

**Short Tandem Repeats**

- Unrelated people almost never have identical DNA profiles (continued)
- The chance of two people having the same DNA profile over all 13 STR arrays is about one in a trillion
- A suspect who differs from the crime scene banding pattern at even one STR locus is exonerated

## Slide 29 {#slide-29}

**DNA Profiling Detects Genetic Differences**

- Investigators use PCR and DNA sequencing to detect genetic differences between individuals.

## Slide 30 {#slide-30}

**STR Comparison**

- Comparing the STR profile of an individual with those of a reference profile reveals what STRs are present in his or her DNA.

## Slide 31 {#slide-31}

**Who belongs to whom?**


## Slide 32 {#slide-32}

**Chapter 13 At a Glance**

- What Is Biotechnology?
- Polymerase Chain Reaction
- Forensics
- Human Genome Project

## Slide 33 {#slide-33}

**Human Genome Project**

- The Human Genome Project was launched in 1990 with the goal of determining the nucleotide sequence of all the DNA in our entire set of genes, our genome
- Findings from the Human Genome Project
- By 2003, molecular biologists, from several countries, sequenced the human genome with 99.99% accuracy

## Slide 34 {#slide-34}

**5000 bases on this page**

- 

## Slide 35 {#slide-35}

**How much data make up the human genome?**

- 3 pallets with 40 boxes per pallet x 5000 pages per box x 5000 bases per page = 3,000,000,000 bases!
- To get accurate
- sequence requires
- 6-fold coverage.
- Now:  Shred 18 pallets
- and reassemble.

## Slide 36 {#slide-36}

**Findings of the Human Genome Project**

- Findings from the Human Genome Project
- There are 19,949 human genes
- Genes make up only 2% of the total DNA
- New genes, including many disease-associated genes, were discovered, but it’s not really known what most of DNA does

## Slide 37 {#slide-37}

**Why the Human Genome Project**

- Why was this project undertaken?
- Knowing the DNA sequence of a novel gene may allow biologists to predict the function of the protein the gene encodes

## Slide 38 {#slide-38}

**Outcomes of the Human Genome Project**

- Knowing the sequence of a gene allows predictions about what the amino acid sequence of the encoded protein will be
- The amino acid sequence can then be compared with proteins whose functions are already known, and if matches are found, inferences can be made about the new gene’s function
- Knowing the nucleotide sequences of human genes will aid in the treatment of many diseases

## Slide 39 {#slide-39}

**Direct to Consumer Sequencing**


## Slide 40 {#slide-40}

**To catch a killer**

- DeAngelo
- 13 victims 1974-1986
- DNA evidence collected from over 50 different SA victims
- PCR helped solve the case
- Distant relative submitted DNA sample
